summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how a flawed encryption function ended up in his codebase and traces its origins across the internet, finding 131 instances of the same broken code being copied and shared. He argues that the entire team bears responsibility for code quality, not just the individual contributor, invoking collective code ownership. The post introduces Scott Hanselman's metaphor of the internet as 'the bathroom wall of code' – a place where random code graffiti of varying quality gets scrawled and mindlessly copied. Atwood concludes by urging developers to apply rigorous peer review to any code snippets absorbed from the internet, as that perfect-looking solution could be dangerous graffiti.
key_points: Team collective ownership means everyone is responsible for every line of code, regardless of who wrote it
The same flawed encryption code was found in 131 different places across the internet, demonstrating how bad code propagates
Internet code snippets are like bathroom wall graffiti – some art, some vandalism, impossible to tell at a glance
Copy-paste code reuse without review spreads security vulnerabilities at scale
Extra-strength peer review is essential for any code absorbed from external sources
money_quotes: I invoke the First Rule of Programming: It's Always Your Fault.
Nobody owns the code, because everybody owns the code.
Yes, I failed. Because the team failed.
Heaven forbid someone copy and paste flawed code from the internet into their project!
This Encrypt() function is like the village bicycle: everybody's had a ride.
People, being people, have gone and scrawled a bunch of random code graffiti all over the damn internet.
That internet code snippet you're looking at, the one that appears to be just what you're looking for, could also be random graffiti scrawled on a bathroom wall.
key_insight: Code snippets spread across the internet like graffiti, and without rigorous peer review, you're just copying someone else's mistakes.
